name: Wakea
description: Wakea, in Hawaiian mythology, is the Sky Father and a primordial ancestor of the Hawaiian people. He is considered the progenitor of the aliʻi (ruling class) and is responsible for the creation of the islands. Wakea's union with Papa, the Earth Mother, is a central creation myth, from which the Hawaiian islands and all life sprung forth. He represents the expansive heavens, the source of light, rain, and fertility. Wakea is associated with governance, order, and the establishment of social structures. His influence extends to agriculture, navigation, and the cycles of life. He is venerated as a powerful and benevolent force, ensuring the prosperity and continuity of the Hawaiian people. Wakea's actions and relationships established the kapu system, a complex set of laws and prohibitions that governed Hawaiian society. He is a figure of immense respect and represents the divine origin of Hawaiian culture and its connection to the natural world. He is sometimes depicted as a bird, symbolizing his connection to the sky and his ability to traverse vast distances. Wakea's legacy continues to shape Hawaiian identity and understanding of their place in the cosmos.
subtitle: Sky Father, Progenitor of the Islands

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Islands
keyMyths.description: Wakea and Papa's union resulted in the birth of the Hawaiian islands. Each island represents a specific stage in their relationship and the development of the world.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Kapu System
keyMyths.description: Wakea's actions and desires led to the establishment of the kapu system. This system defined social order and established prohibitions to maintain balance.

keyMyths.title: Wakea and Hoʻohokukalani
keyMyths.description: Wakea's relationship with his daughter Hoʻohokukalani is a complex myth. This relationship resulted in the birth of Hāloa, the first Hawaiian and the ancestor of all people.
keyMyths.source: Fornander, An Account of the Polynesian Race
